statement: In April 2024, former Italian Prime Minister Enrico Letta presented his report 'Much More Than a Market' to the European Council, commissioned by the Belgian EU Presidency. The report assessed the state of the EU single market 30 years after its creation and proposed reforms for its future. Key findings included: (1) the single market remains incomplete in services, with only about 25% of services cross-border trade potential realised; (2) the EU's financial markets are fragmented, with savings of approximately €300 billion per year leaving the EU for investment in US assets; (3) the digital single market lacks scale, with fragmented national regulations hampering European tech companies; (4) energy market integration remains insufficient. The report proposed creating a 'fifth freedom' of the single market — free movement of research, innovation, and data — alongside the existing four freedoms (goods, services, capital, persons). It also called for harmonising corporate taxation, creating a European savings and investment product, and strengthening EU-level procurement for defence and security.
source_name: Letta, Enrico — 'Much More Than a Market: Speed, Security, Solidarity', Report to the European Council (April 2024)
caveats: The Letta report preceded the Draghi report and both were intended to feed into the EU's post-2024 election policy agenda. Implementation depends on the new European Commission and member state cooperation. The report's recommendations on financial market integration and corporate tax harmonisation face significant political resistance from member states including Ireland, Luxembourg, and the Netherlands.

related_entries.statement: In September 2024, former ECB President Mario Draghi presented his report 'The Future of European Competitiveness' to the European Commission. The report identified a significant productivity and competitiveness gap between the EU and the United States and China, noting that: (1) EU GDP per capita grew approximately 1% per year slower than the US over the past two decades; (2) EU firms face energy prices 2–3 times higher than US competitors and natural gas prices 4–5 times higher; (3) only 4 of the world's top 50 tech companies are European; (4) the EU needs approximately €750–800 billion in additional annual investment (roughly 4.4–4.7% of EU GDP) to close the gap in innovation, decarbonisation, and defence. Key recommendations included: creating truly integrated capital markets (completing the Capital Markets Union), reforming competition policy to allow European champions, coordinating industrial policy across member states, and drastically simplifying regulation. The report explicitly warned that without radical action, Europe faces 'a slow agony' of declining competitiveness and living standards.

The European Union is Iceland's largest trading partner. In 2024, approximately 50% of Iceland's goods exports and 60% of goods imports by value were with EU member states. Marine products (primarily fish) constitute the largest single export category to the EU.

The EU has adopted a vast body of legislation since its founding. As of end 2024, the EUR-Lex database records approximately 146,700 regulations and 4,600 directives adopted since 1958, totalling over 151,000 binding legal acts. During the EEA period (1994–2024), the EU adopted roughly 57,100 regulations (averaging 1,842 per year) and 2,626 directives (85 per year). Legislative output has declined from a peak of approximately 4,000+ regulations per year in the late 1980s to around 1,200 per year in 2019–2023.
